specification.topic-statement: The database session management module creates new session records and retrieves the most recently started active session from the sessions table.

scope.in-scope: Session record insertion with database-generated ID retrieval, and active session lookup with tiebreaking.
scope.boundary: The underlying PostgreSQL connection and query execution are managed externally. This topic only specifies the SQL operations and result handling.
When sessions are created and how they are used during application startup is covered by the session startup specification.

field.name: started_at
field.note: set to the current time by the database on insert

field.name: ended_at
field.note: null for active sessions; set when the session ends

behavior.session-creation:
Inserts a new row into the sessions table using default column values, causing the database to populate the started_at timestamp with the current time and ended_at with null. The generated session ID is retrieved in the same query using a RETURNING clause.
The returned ID string is parsed as a decimal 64-bit signed integer. If the query returns no rows, the process aborts (this would indicate an internal database invariant violation). The session ID is returned to the caller.
behavior.active-session-lookup:
Queries for the most recently started session whose ended_at column is null. Results are ordered by started_at descending, with the session ID used as a secondary sort key to break ties among sessions created in the same database transaction.
If no rows are returned, the output session ID is set to zero and success is returned. A session ID of zero is the sentinel indicating that no active session was found.
If the query returns multiple rows, only the first row (the most recent) is used. The remaining rows are ignored without error.
The caller must distinguish session ID zero (no active session) from a valid session ID. Zero is never a valid database-generated session ID.
